design_philosophy.core_loop: Select robot master stage → defeat enemies and navigate platforming challenges → defeat boss to acquire master weapon → use new weapon to access alternate paths and defeat subsequent bosses → progress to fortress → defeat final boss
design_philosophy.primary_tension: Resource management (health, weapon energy) versus pattern recognition (boss attack sequences); non-linear stage selection creates strategic weapon ordering puzzle
design_philosophy.player_fantasy: Mega Man as adaptable combat robot who inherits powers from defeated enemies; gradual power fantasy through collected master weapons and rush adaptors
design_philosophy.what_makes_it_opinionated: Game doubles down on established NES Mega Man formula rather than innovating; introduces alternate pathways within stages tied to rush adaptors (Jet vs Power form), adding optional route complexity; Energy Balancer automatically manages weapon resources, reducing inventory micromanagement friction

difficulty.lives_system: Extra lives collectible throughout stages; life loss returns player to stage start
difficulty.continues_system: Continue system present; specific continue mechanics not detailed in sources
difficulty.checkpointing: Stages lack mid-stage checkpoints; boss encounters appear to restart from stage entry point upon death
difficulty.player_agency_in_difficulty: Player selects stage order freely, enabling strategic weapon sequencing based on boss weaknesses; alternate pathways optional (only one provides Beat circuit board, suggesting risk-reward choice); rush adaptors trade mobility for utility (Jet prevents charged shots; both prevent sliding)

progression.upgrade_system: Master Weapons acquired from defeated Robot Masters (8 total); Rush Adaptors (Jet Mega Man for flight, Power Mega Man for melee/block breaking); Energy Balancer for weapon resource management; Beat helper bird from collecting 4 circuit board letters across stages
progression.unlock_mechanism: Alternate stage pathways require specific rush adaptors; Beat assembly requires collecting all 4 letter boards distributed across different stages (only one pathway per stage yields a letter); Master Weapons unlock access to specific boss weaknesses
replayability_hooks: Non-linear stage selection creates different weapon progression paths
Alternate pathways in stages tied to rush adaptor choice
Beat letter board collection encourages thorough stage exploration
Strategy gaming around optimal boss encounter order based on weapon advantages

world_building_techniques: Tournament framing device: Global Robot Alliance hosts First Annual Robot Tournament to establish peacekeeping robot
Mr. X reveal as series manipulator masks Dr. Wily as recurring antagonist (subversion/continuation of established lore)
Multi-stage fortress progression mirrors escalating threat (Mr. X's fortress → Dr. Wily's new stronghold)
International robot master roster (tournament entrants from 'around the globe') creates cosmopolitan setting

boss_design.philosophy: Eight Robot Masters present distinct attack patterns and mobility; each weak to specific master weapon from another robot, creating strategic interdependency; fortress bosses (Mr. X/Dr. Wily) represent final confrontation escalation
notable_bosses: Centaur Man (unused aerial throwing animation suggests incomplete attack pattern design)
Flame Man (animation frame reuse indicates iterative development)
Yamato Man (forces player to disrupt attack pattern via Power Mega Man charged attack for unique interaction)
Mr. X (requires pattern learning before reveal)
Dr. Wily (second confrontation after Mr. X revelation)

faction_indicators: Global Robot Alliance as peacekeeping faction opposing Dr. Wily
Mr. X Foundation as mysterious antagonistic organization
International robot master tournament suggests national/regional representation
danger_signals: Dr. Wily's repeated attacks established in prior entry (Mega Man 5) necessitated Global Robot Alliance formation
Mr. X's infiltration and reprogramming of tournament participants signals coordinated threat
Fortress architecture (dual strongholds) indicates entrenched threat requiring escalating confrontation
environmental_storytelling: Tournament setting establishes competitive rather than military framing
Mr. X's fortress design and presence suggest technological sophistication
Stage design reflects individual robot master themes (implied by weapon inheritance system)
Rush adaptor modifications indicate Mega Man's adaptive capability as design philosophy
